sentenceFrame: The moth is in the bath.
celebration: th leaf #3 goes up. 🎉 SENTENCE GLUE EXPLODES — this, that, then, them and with become decodable in one week, and for the first time the class can build a REAL joined-up sentence. The single most important EAL sound of Level 2.

songs.title: Th-Th-That!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
Th! Th! Th-th-th!
(th… th… th…)

[Hook]
Th! Th! Th-th-th!
Two sounds, ONE team!
Th-th-that! Th-th-that!
It's THAT! (that one! that!)

[Verse 2]
Th! Th! Th-th-th!
The soft one buzzes — feel it!
Th-th-this! Th-th-then!
This and then! (buzz… buzz…)

[Verse 3]
Th! Th! Th-th-th!
The quiet one hushes — feel it!
Th-th-thin! Th-th-moth!
Thin as a moth! (hush… hush…)

[Verse 4]
Th! Th! Th-th-th!
In the bath, on the path!
Th-th-bath! Th-th-path!
Splish in the bath! (splash!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not TH!
(th… th… th…)

[Outro]
Th-th-that! Th-th-this!
That and this! (this and that!)
Th! Th! Th-th-th!
(with… with… with…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: th makes TWO sounds and this song teaches BOTH — the VOICED /ð/ (this, that, then) buzzes on the tongue (verse 2: hand on the throat, feel it hum), and the VOICELESS /θ/ (thin, moth, bath, path) is a quiet air-hush (verse 3). The tongue peeks between the teeth for both. NEVER the letter names 'tee-aitch', and NEVER a /d/ or /s/ or /f/ substitute — that substitution IS the EAL error this whole week fights. Verses 2 and 3 deliberately contrast buzz versus hush.

songs.title: The Moth in the Bath
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
The moth is in the bath!
The moth is in the bath!
Where is the moth? (where? where?)
The moth is in the bath!

[Verse 2]
This moth! That moth!
Thin moth in the bath!
Splish and splash! (splash! splash!)
The moth is in the bath!

[Verse 3]
Then… up! Up! (flap! flap!)
The moth is on the path!
Where is the moth? (where? where?)
The moth is with Chick!

[Outro]
The moth… the bath… (drip… drip…)
A potato?! (POTATO!)
In the bath?! (splash!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'The moth is in the bath.' — the first frame stacked with th words. Verse 2 fires the new glue (this/that/thin), verse 3 lands 'then' and 'with' — every th word the week teaches appears in the story. Whisper the dripping outro, potato hits loud, splash closes it.

spreads.artNote: An old claw-foot bath brimming with water, lit from above, steam curling off. Still and inviting.
A soft grey moth flutters into the spotlight, dusty wings catching the light. Gentle, not scary.

spreads.text: The moth is in the bath.
spreads.artNote: The moth has landed on the water's surface, wings held delicately up out of the wet. Tiny ripples spread around it.
Close-up of the moth, wings thin and papery, translucent in the light. The word 'thin' made visible.
The moth beats its wings, flinging tiny droplets everywhere — a joyful little storm inside the bath. Comedy of a very small splash.
Chick perched on the rim of the bath beside the moth, watching kindly, tiny wing extended in company. Two small friends.

spreads.text: Then the moth is on the path.
spreads.artNote: The moth lifts off and settles on a garden path of stepping-stones beyond the bath, wings dry now, glowing softly.
The moth rides gently on the nose of a fluffy round white sheep with kind dark eyes, both looking pleased. Chick trots alongside.
A single potato bobs in the middle of the bath now, water rings around it, a tiny rubber-duck expression of surprise on Chick at the rim. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it.

sentences: The moth is in the bath.
Then the moth is on the path.
extras: PATTERN TREE: leaf #3. Read this/that/then/with aloud and the th leaf joins sh and ch.
TWO SOUNDS, ONE LEAF: sort the words into buzz (voiced /ð/: this, that, then, them, with) and hush (voiceless /θ/: thin, moth, bath, path, math). A hand on the throat is the test.
SENTENCE GLUE: with these five words the class writes its first joined sentence — 'This moth is thin and that moth is on the path.' Build it in the pocket chart.

EAL minimal-pair drill (THE keystone): th vs t and th vs s — thin/tin, thin/sin, that/sat, thick/sick, path/pat, math/mass (oral), bath/bat. Mandarin-L1 children have NEITHER th sound; use a mirror so the child watches the tongue peek out. Do this every day this week.
Moveable alphabet: build th welded, then this, that, then, with, moth, bath.

teacherFocus: THE MOST IMPORTANT EAL WEEK OF LEVEL 2. th makes two sounds — the voiced buzz /ð/ (this, that, then, them, with) and the voiceless hush /θ/ (thin, moth, bath, path, math). Teach both from one sandpaper digraph; a hand on the throat sorts them. Mandarin has NEITHER sound, so children default to /s/, /d/, /f/ or /t/ — 'sink' for 'think', 'dat' for 'that'. Run the minimal-pair drill EVERY day (thin/tin, thin/sin, that/sat, path/pat) with a mirror so the child watches the tongue tip peek between the teeth. The reward is huge: this/that/then/them/with are the connective tissue of English, so this is the week sentences stop being lists and start joining up — celebrate the first real joined-up sentence.
